commentBody:
Now that was a fine Friday.
Thank you, Zachary.
I don’t know how you all are making your s’mores but I’ve never know one to catch fire. The marshmallow yes but the entire s’more? Never.
please  explain Tahoe runner- mac. crossing manga .. no idea what the "m" was. had all the other letters, Is Jibjab still around? i remember when it was super-hot

Awesome Friday puzzle, with all sorts of twists and misdirections.  I particularly liked “direction in many a spaghetti western” for THATAWAY and “Totally missing the big picture” for NEARSIGHTED.  As an ex lawyer, ARGUEDACASE was the one longer clue I was able to get right away, and without that substantial foothold I might still be floundering around.

Much more like a Saturday than a Friday IMO.  Too many proper nouns all bunched together at the bottom.  DAN, ARTUR, CERES, and RUDI are 4 of 6 consecutive down answers in that area, and they are all essentially unguessable.  I had no clue about the MANGA factlet, so 5 of the 6 consecutive answers were blanks for me, and the Across answers in that area could have been many things. (eg, "Kitchen gizmo"?  Really?  Something that vague is a Saturday clue, not a Friday one IMO.).  

I personally found the bottom part of the puzzle very frustrating.

Excellent Friday puzzle. Lots of TIL moments and I needed a fair bit of luck on some of the crossings.

Not to get too political but I had SHADY and SLIMY for 25A but those didn't seem to work.

I don't remember the last time I disliked a puzzle so much. Just, yuck. I don't even know how to be constructive about it, but very little felt fresh. 

Not every puzzle is for everyone, so I'm hoping for a more enjoyable time tomorrow.

I hope folks found this a worthy Friday. 
That south section was so hard for me. I spent a third of my time there, even with two of the long across crosses. 

Tahoe runner. SKI. no ELK. nope. 
I really don’t remember RUDI. I was like, was Rosie, spelled Rosi?  what daughter Judy a robot?  

Thankfully no flyspecking.  
Great Friday cluing to complement a nice grid. At least methinks. 

Football and gambling clues. Sounds good to me
Whaddya know - there actually is another pianist called ANTON Rubinstein.

Annoying puzzle in many ways.

MAC I only associated with Tahoe from muscle memory. Had to look to the comments to see the connection.

Don't understand how THATAWAY applies to a Spaghetti Western. Unless characters saying that is some kind of established trope, it's a pretty weak clue.

The whole ATALANTA crossing was filled with potential Naticks. And don't even get me started on 12A. Ms. Swiatek has a mediocre 6 months and she loses crossword privileges? Not fair!
The South/Southeast block was really no fun. As DJ pointed out, way too many proper nouns. The rest of the puzzle was fine, but no great shakes.

I thought this was a fine Friday with a lot of interesting entries, some of which were new to me, KDRAMA, JIBJAB, and ARMBAR.  SAMMICH certainly made me smile. I liked the combo of THATAWAY and THISONE, and as much as GIRLBOSS is distasteful, it was at least next to a real BOSS woman from mythology, ATALANTA. I had forgotten that origin story but read about her if you want know the rest. I appreciated that even some of the gimmes were clever and funny—firs and beeches instead of furs and beaches.
Finally, I think CORER and PARER are the latest KEALOA for me, I always seem to guess wrong.

Two days in a row, I haven't been able to get to the comments section from within the Games app. I'm unable to click the little comment bubble counter in the bottom right corner of the column. I have to get here from the web.

Anyone else seeing this?
That was a better Friday (even if the TREES clue escaped from a Monday puzzle).

You had me at Artur. Is there any finer interpreter of the late Mozart piano concerti, particularly 21 & 23?

This was a right Friday of a puzzle. Much fun, loved the cluing. [Unlit?] SOBER ... and THATAWAY! Wow! I'd imagine some Italian-Americans might bristle, but it's all in good fun.

I was convinced [Computer runs] was SPAM. I mean, if a computer had the runs, wouldn't the result be spam? 

I feel uneasy about SAMMICH, bit too close to baby talk (I have a friend who's currently not speaking to me because I yelled at him about calling something a "sammie."). Standards and decorum must be maintained.

So, if cold neuralgia is Brain Freeze, would Brainstorm be wet neuralgia, Braindrain dry neuralgia, and Brainfart windy neuralgia? Asking for a friend.
Proper Friday, sir….not just for show.

Ok, I hope you guys are happy.

There's been so much about the archives, I decided I really needed to go WAY back and see what I could find.  So I went to 2003.

I done about ten puzzles, and I had absolutely no idea for 90% of the clues without a cross.  None.  Nothing at all comes to me for [Blouse with a sailor collar]--MIDDY???  A two part clue of [With 7-Across, something to start with] is BANANA DAIQUIRI???  Even for the easier ones like [Evict], BOUNCE took forever and a day.

I know, I know, crosses, right? Sure, like the cross [What taxis do]?  Which is HONK?  I didn't find a lot of crosses to help me out.

So now I'm in a frame of mind that I simply can't get anything, so when I tried today's puzzle I struggled because I was in a mindset of failure.

Now I can't do the archives because I'm too dumb, and I can't do the current ones because I'm traumatized.  

I hope you're all happy. 

Verklempt I am.  I bet I'm the verklemptest guy in Minnesota.  And it's not a fun type of verklempt, I can assure you of that.
Good puzzle. No idea what a kdrama is but don’t care

first acrosses on my first pass were reminiscent of the hard Fridays of yore. I like when the grid stays mostly empty for a while. lower hanging fruit in the downs and higher numbered clues.

Tahoe runner crossing dwarf planet got me. thought of a mat as a runner of sorts in the footwell of a Chevy Tahoe. Teres seemed plausible. my MacBook is too old to update anymore, though I'm sure there was plenty of media coverage of Tahoe when "liquid glass" was coming in for scorn. fair play.

enjoyed the puzzle. hawt grid design and no entries that bothered me all that much. a few fun ones even.

So this evening I learned that the definition of "reduplicative" is more expansive than I thought. (JIBJAB)

And it took me an ungodly amount of time before I realized I was working on a "Tahoe runner."  (MAC)

But please, dear puzzlers: DON'T RUB IT IN!

Misdirection in many a Migos song?

DAT WAY

I know others have brought this up but I just had a chance to watch the Elspeth episode about the murder of a crossword editor. It really is a hoot and every character is an amalgam of just about every crossword commenter here (including myself). Watch it if you can just for a laugh. Only nit  is part of the plot hinges on someone else editing the puzzle the weekend before its release and people complaining how bad it was (and not just for the clueing). We all know an editor might alter the clues last minute (and even that’s a couple of weeks out for the NYT) but certainly not the grid entries. Gotcha, Elsbeth!
Loved this puzzle, some thought-provoking entries that weren’t just obscure sports references and very few of the “usual suspects” (apart from ORA.) A very enjoyable way to pass a Friday lunchtime.

The last time I saw anything from JIBJAB was probably in the aughts, and at the time I thought they were just a sarcastic parody video site, so never visited it.

Got hung up on 'teeth' for choppers, given my recent acquisition of dentures, but finally figured out that it didn't match the crosses well. It did match enough to get me BRAIN FREEZE, and that got me back on the correct path. I was also A BIT Off on 36A, and I started with SAMMie, which quickly proved to be too short, before entering the right answer.

Good Friday; thanks, Zachary!
Really wanted 29A to be ecdysiast, but it wasn’t long enough.
Isn't it Arthur Rubinstein, not ARTUR?

Speaking of they went thataway, buckle up for two rides on the comments wagon, based on how folks fared with this mighty fine Friday ...TRUE GRIT style...

"When I have bought and paid for something I will have my way."
Mattie Ross

"Well, it's the real article! Genuine, double-rectified bust head. Aged in the keg."
Rooster Cogburn

Guess what lucky chums, we get another one tomorrow! Remember to keep an eye on them marshmallows, afore they go and fall off that skewer . . .
Guessed wrongly that Lauren HUTTON was a Tenenbaum…and took way too long wondering what a BUTT had to do with hitting 22…sigh.

I'm sure somebody thought this was a Monday, but I found it a proper challenge, and for the right reasons,  too, mostly - misdirection and indirect clueing. I needed Saturday time to solve the puzzle.

I was tempted to look up a few things in the trivia-heavy NE corner - it was the only bit of the grid I did not enjoy, as being stumped by arcana never feels great. ATALANTA was my only gimme there. In the end I managed to deal with it on my own - I just checked Wikipedia for Morse code terminology: I'm familiar with dots and dashes (also in Polish: kropka/kreska), and even though DITs and dahs appeared in these puzzles before, I just can never remember them, as they seem non5en5ic@I to my Polish brain.
For longer than I'd like to admit I was convinced it was a MOM doing school runs in a Chevrolet Tahoe
That is not what BAG is slang for…

Perfect puzzle for a Friday.  I didn't try to overthink it, just dropped guesses in, went back and took them out when the crosses said they were wrong, and there the right answers were; e.g, had "Fold" for Hit 22 and thought for one wild moment that maybe I'd misspelled Phooey all these years, then had the Doh! moment and realized it was BUST.  Taking out the seat-holders wasn't always necessary—some of them were actually right. 

I had a wonderful time solving it. I suspect there will be the usual MOANers. but I thought it was ALLOK and then some.

Thanks, Zachary David Levy, for a very cool puzzle.

As a neurologist, "cold neuralgia" opened this wide open for me. However, no one calls it that. For anyone with any pain above the neck, please refer to the ichd3 (international classification of headache disorders, 3rd edition) which is available for free online. BRAIN FREEZE is actually cold stimulus headache, classified as 4.5.

I am intimately familiar with this. About 20 years ago, for (I think) my wife's 25th birthday, my friend brought over his own home fashioned beer bong. He had professional levers installed to ensure that we could fully control the flow. However, being the snob that I am, I declined bonging any beers. Instead, I decided to bong a frozen Margarita. Yes I did it. WORST BRAIN FREEZE EVER. DO NOT RECOMMEND.

Also, @Steve L, this should make me older than 20...

Random question for the chat regarding the NYT Games Puzzlemania book. 

The crossword on page 144, titled Duly Noted. 

The shaded theme answers. 

If anyone could explain their meaning, I would be grateful.

commentBody:
Who here knew that ORZO was Italian for barley? I resisted that change through many iterations because I was sure it meant "rice". Unless it began as rice and then expanded its meaning to include barley as well.
But between Spanish "arroz" and French "orge", which one would you think was a sibling to 'orzo'?!

Simply awful. I can hardly remember a puzzle where I was on such an opposite wavelength to the author. Too many clunkers to list, every other clue was too clever by half or completely contrived. Onwards

Trivia tidbit: 

The dwarf planet Ceres was discovered by Giuseppe Piazzi on 1 January 1801, the same day that the Act of Union 1800 was completed - which brought Britain and Ireland together as the United Kingdom, while abolishing the Parliament of Ireland. 

So Ceres is as old as the UK. But only one of them has a core of water and occasionally remodels its surface by shooting cryovolcanoes of muddy ice into its thin atmosphere.

Now *that* was a Friday puzzle. In the NE, BUnk before BULL and not knowing IGA as clued, DIT, ATALANTA or HUSTON forced me to do some deductive reasoning. GIRL BOSS gave me hives when I was forced to write it in, but at least it was clued as reductive. Then there was the SE. SAMMIes before SAMMICH (both awful), and suv before MAC, slowed things down for a while, making me work for it. Thanks for an excellent Friday puzzle, Zachary. Very much needed. To make my morning even better, the great Joe Strummer with RUDIe Can't Fail: https://youtu.be/dIVjSZKHKlE?si=ovjcmDdK3SOAbf-W

This was a really tough one for me. First pass through the Acrosses: one answer filled. Next pass through the Downs: one more answer. Third pass and beyond: a very slow fill, requiring a few lookups and a whole lot of creative thinking. In other words, a stellar Friday puzzle. I hope everyone who has been asking for harder puzzles is happy, because this one tried to break my brain. (I’m not complaining.)

Also: my husband has a very annoying habit of referring to a sandwich as a “sammich”. He’s an intelligent person; I don’t know how this seeped into his vocabulary. I am not going to tell him that his obnoxious quirk made the NYT crossword.
I'm not enough of a native U.S. person to solve this one without cheating (JIBJAB, IGA, SAMMICH, BUBBA, THATAWAY?) but it was nice to have a challenge!
“Tahoe runner” is MAC? Sheesh.

Just a brilliant puzzle. There is nothing better than giving a crossword a first pass, being able to fill only a handful of clues and then slowly bringing the full grid to completion. Thanks for such a Friday morning treat Zachary!

Please fix the link to Wordplay column so it opens in a browser like it used to. After the recent update, it opens inside the app so if you need a hint or want to read the column while solving, for any reason, you can't have the puzzle and the column open at the same time.
Kdramas and Rubi did me in.
I loved the flow of today's puzzle. Very much enjoyed the simple phrases as they appeared:"Just for show", "A bit odd". I did need a few look ups but I'm ok with that. Please , "Don't rub it in!"
This puzzle had more clunkers than usual.

Some tactical revealing (eg JIB JAB, BUBBA, SAMMICH) opened this one out a bit, although it was still hard, with quite a few words I hadn't heard of.

Maybe it's just me and the people I've known, but I've never had a brunch, and had no idea a HAM OMELETTE was part of it. 

I'm glad lots of people found it a suitable Friday.

This was a toughie for me (finally). I had to look a few up in the northeast corner, including DIT.
I guessed ATALANTA early on, but doubted myself, because -- like many of us -- I grew up listening to the album "Free to Be You and Me." In Marlo Thomas and Alan Alda's version, that heroine is raised by King John, not bears.

I hope Sam's photo was not implying this puzzle was a PISA cake. It sure didn't lean my way.

Going out for brunch soon, inlaws are in town. No sammiches or ham omelettes, more likely to be saugage and grits with my eggs.

Friday? 

Nope.

Fri-yay.

Fri-licious.

Fri-rific. 


Fanx fer dat.

This is Zachary’s first themeless puzzle out of ten in the Times, and I hope it’s not his last.  

It gave me the perfect mix of whoosh, slow motion, and in-between.  I had fun, my brain got its workout, and there were sweet periods of even-ness to amplify the extemes.

What a gorgeous black square grid design, looking like two hooks, or maybe two J’s.  I’ll go with the latter, since the puzzle begins with the sing-song JIBJAB.

A sweet moment:  Being stumped by [Totally missing the big picture?], then after getting NEARSIGHTED through crosses, with a big “Hah!,” grokking that clue and bowing down to its cleverness.

Lovely answers in DON’T RUB IT IN (a debut), THATAWAY, and SAMMICH.  Lots of zing, with all six triple-stack answers in the SE and NW having appeared in the 80 years of Times puzzles but four times or less.  Wow!

The uber-low number of black squares (28, ten less than a typical Monday) allows for a sea of white, and Zachary filled it with interest and junk-be-gone.  

Man, Zachary, you hit my sweet spot with this.  Thank you, and more please!

I usually enjoy a challenge but for some reason this puzzle just made me angry. Can't explain it, and not blaming anyone but myself. That's just the feeling it invoked. Oh well. Time for a mimosa and eggs benedict. Cheers!

For once, a Friday puzzle worthy of being a Friday puzzle. Lots of head-scratching and the overwhelming feeling that I would never be able to finish this one.  There were many passes that yielded nothing. MAC, ARMBAR, and KDRAMAS were a few of the ones that slowed me way down, and I had EXOTIC DANCER before I realized that it had to be EROTIC ARTIST, whatever that is, since I don't think it's a term that's in the vernacular. Eventually I was able to finish it without looking anything up. But I sure was grateful for the challenge! I had to read the comments to find out what the first three were, even after I had solved the puzzle.
Love the column photo of the beautiful new ballroom!
Just right for a friday, took me a little under 10 minutes

Very enjoyable puzzle! 
Note: if you ever catch a SMORE on fire you're doing it very very wrong - only the shmallow goes anywhere near the fire, and if that catches fire then you're doing it very right!
I was stuck for a bit because I had Arcturus/Arcturos as the bear-raised figure.  Isn't the story that his mother was a bear?
Notice that when a proper Friday challenge shows up, no one complains about it being too easy?
Solvers who found this a very hard Friday (compared to Fridays over the past couple of years) are in good company. https://xwstats.com/puzzles/2026-03-27

I liked this Friday puzzle a whole bunch! Appropriately, I mostly doubted my crossings in the IDOUBTIT region, where I didn’t know some of the suspects.

Warning: Canadian-based reference:
I’ve never seen “Squid Game” or “The Red Sleeve” - 
are KD-RAMAS, like spaghetti westerns, part of the Pasta Cinematic Universe?

And finally: surely, we all should prefer *fewer* than 30 black squares?
A really good Thursday workout. Well done!
Oops, Friday. Being retired I tend to lose track of what day it is 😐

Somehow, the column's Tricky Clues/Entries are never the things that stump me.  I managed to get K DRAMA (which I just googled) thanks to the 22A crossing, but where I ran aground was over there in the NE corner.  

I had never heard the gossip about ATALANTA's upbringing, but I was stuck at 21A's Malarkey/21d's Hit 22.... and 28S's costar....

I ended up with PUTT for 21D (I don't golf or gamble), PULP for the 21A Malarkey (pulp fiction, eh?)  which helped me get actress HUTTON (remember her? Lauren? Tall, space between her front teeth, way back when....?)  Well, I tried.

So, Zach Levy can hang a head on the trophy wall; go ahead, RUB IT IN.  To the victor belong the spoils.

Was painfully stuck on 48D for a while - even with 47A and 62A filled in - until I went with the Polish spelling. Great puzzle even though I got slower than my average for the first time in weeks! Thanks!

To disambiguate, ORZO is a name for a tiny shape of pasta, that looks like rice. Apparently, in Italy, though, it means "barley." From Wikipedia: [Orzo (/ˈɔːrzoʊ, ˈɔːrtsoʊ/,[1][2][3] Italian: [ˈɔrdzo]; lit. 'barley'; from Latin hordeum), also known in Italy as risoni (Italian: [riˈzoːni]; 'large [grains of] rice'), and popular in Greek cuisine as kritharaki (κριθαράκι), is a form of short-cut pasta shaped like a large grain of rice.[4] Orzo is made from flour,[5] often with semolina. [The name orzo is common for this pasta shape in North America, but less so in Italy, where the word usually means 'barley'.[citation needed]] See a photo here: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Orzo

Oh my goodness...this is the Friday workout we've all been hoping for. I thoroughly enjoyed every second of this puzzle. I thought I'd need Google at a couple of junctures, but being patient was rewarded with AHA moments.

A proper Friday challenge! Thanks, editors! More please! 

Here’s hoping this is the start to a great puzzle weekend.

Yes, now that is a Friday!

I loved "No no" for YES especially. The NW was the last to fall for me. I couldn't figure out what the heck "cold neuralgia" was--hypothermia? Nope. I also got thrown by Anjelica HUSTON, forgetting that her name is not spelled like the city.

My single nit is PARER. I simply do not believe it is a thing, and I will die on this hill. It's my personal OREO: whenever I see it, I cringe slightly. There is no parer in my kitchen. A paring knife, yes. A peeler, yes. A parer? No!

Thanks to Zachary David Levy for a fantastic puzzle!
Excellent!

Hoo-ah! Thank you, Zachary. Just what we’ve been wishing for. That feeling after working the NW corner for 10 minutes that I might not get it. Maybe, I’ll need a small “help”. Nope! It finally came together. Maybe it’s a little masochistic and I love it when a Friday or Saturday takes over 25 minutes.
Work of crossword art
Boy oh BOY. I hung on to “sammie” for waaaaay too long.

I LOVED this puzzle! I went into it with dread because of its closed off corners, and I got nowhere pretty fast until STENO, ORZO and WEE lead me to BRAINFREEZE and JUSTFORSHOW which opened up things for me. Those got me to JIBJABs which are not your average ecard! They’re way more fun. 

NEARSIGHTED stumped me way longer than it should have considering how nearsighted I am, and have been since 3 years old. I couldn’t think of a word shorter than short that fit, until I got DAN by guessing which male name with an A in the middle fit there best lol. 

THATAWAY and Zsa Zsa’s MARRIAGEs gave me back to back chuckles. 

Loved the Rebel/OBEY opposites for the same reasons I loved its cross’s firs/beeches vs furs/beeches for TREES; the latter being my new favorite clue of all time lol. 

I could see George sitting in front of RUDI in my head but I needed the UDI to actually remember the name, but I loved the nostalgia. 

Just a really fun puzzle!

A good work out. the grid was barely connected in its regions. Without much of a start in the NW I filled in the right side more easily which meant solving the bottom from the ends. The irony of filling in MAC as the last entry while the image of Lake Tahoe runs in the background of my Mac gave me a chuckle. I had tried SKI and GMC. I knew the latter was a stretch as it's a Chevy so I wasn't wedded to the answer.I'm not familiar with IGA and ALL OK? feels a bit unnatural to me. I enjoyed learning some trivia about ATALANTA, CERES and ORZO today.

First, the obvious:
This was an excellent, challenging Friday puzzle.  Nice long entries, fresh fill, good cluing.
Thank you, Zachary David Levy.

Second, a touch of ambivalence:
What did people think about the grid?
I'm not a constructor, and I don't know much about "flow" and "connectivity", but I like the snaking tangent-curve, that "flowed from the SW to the NE.
On the other hand, I'm no fan of sections that are essentially separated from the rest of the puzzle, making them big Minis (or Midis?).  I'm looking at you NW and SE.

Nit du jour:
Like many, I had EROTIC dancer before ARTIST.
I guess this is a personal nit, but I'd like to reserve the term "ARTIST" for museum-quality work.  I think of fine ART as the stuff that stands at the pinnacle of Civilization, and thereby justifies all the abuses that have gone on in history (war, slavery, castes, etc.) that has  been "necessary" to make Civilization possible.
Art, poetry, literature, philosophy.  Without those, we're all in the gutter...
(whether or not any of us are looking at the stars).

Last night, on Jeopardy, I was able to answer a question that none of the three contestants could come up with. That happens sometimes. And always a kick. (Unfortunately, I can't remember what the question was. Maybe someone who streams Jeopardy can help me out.)

It would have been pretty dumb if any of those contestants had missed the Final Jeopardy category, since two of the three words in the answer were in the clue--

Clue: "When it was founded in northern Minnesota in 1902, this company had one goal--to mine for corundum; it has since expanded."

Answer: 3M (Minnesota Mining and Manufacturing Company)
None of them missed it.

There is a very smart champion on Jeopardy now. He is going for his eleventh win and always wears orange, a "bureaucrat and law student."

Apparently, Jeopardy is starting s spin-off version of the game which will debut on youtube on March 31???? Not sure how that will work...
I loved it, thanks ZDL! Conversationally conversational, conservatively nonconservative, and unconventional conventional. Bravo. Thanks for the lead off hit!

Has anyone done one of those national diagrams on the usage of SAMMICH/SAMMIE?

I lived in Tennessee for a while many years ago.  Those words were all over the place there, with the preference, I think being for SAMMIE.  I remember thinking, "I've never heard that before," but it was immediately inferable what they meant.
But that's the only place I know which had a real love for those terms.

I'd love to hear others experience with those words and where they've heard them used and how frequently.
Can anyone explain to me how a "no no" is a yes?
Anyone else have, EXOTICDANCER for 29 Across? Anyone? Anyone?

Plus one for this! Super annoying to not be able to refer to the grid while reading the column and comments. Also while the app works in dark mode, the column doesn't, which is practically an act of aggression on the part of the Times. Comments work in dark mode though. Very wonky.

Great puzzle, and I was impressed by the ability to construct it with so few black squares. I particularly liked the cluing. “Rebel’s opposite” really set me up to look for a noun, and I was scratching my head thinking of a four letter word for patriot or loyalist, when the crossers led me to OBEY. Great misdirect - it worked even though I am generally on the lookout for ambiguities in verb tenses and verb/noun double meanings.
Just WOW -- thank you, Zachary, for this incredible Friday workout. It also happened to be my 300th streak and you sure made me earn it! Well done -- S'MORE of these, please!😊

Took me 20 minutes longer than a normal Friday.       The top part did indeed make my brain freeze.   

Just happy to survive this one and continue my absolutely no look-ups policy streak to almost 10 weeks.
This one was beyond my reach cause I didn't understand half the answers even after filling in the puzzle.

Another upvote on how wonderfully challenging this puzzle was. And such clever clues. This is what I hope for in a Friday-Saturday. Kudos!

Maybe I'm a masochist, but I love that sense of dread when I just can't figure a corner out (the southeast today), and then the elation when one good guess makes it all fall into place.

"Firs and beeches, but not furs and beaches" for TREES nicely exemplifies what some of us have been expressing. It's a cute clue, and would be fine on a Monday. But on Friday, some of us want challenge, not just cuteness. 

"Some acyclic graphs" would be a gimme for me, but would be a much better *Friday* clue for TREES.

The puzzle was fine, but felt like another themeless Wednesday to me.

MAC x DAN x MANGA x ARTUR x  CERES x RUDI is a truly insane section. pretty easy to find where the puzzler gave up and mailed it in.
way too much trivie - this puzzle didn't want to be solved and was not enjoyable.
Not mad about EROTIC ARTISTS - a little clumsy. But the triple stack on the lower right more than made up for it. Pure Puzzle Poetry. Well done.

I really hate that No No means YES. I understand what it's getting at here, but in my woke, feminist world, No never means Yes. Especially if you say it more than once. It could have said "Not a No". It was the last answer I entered because I kept hoping it could turn out differently.
I love a puzzle that stretches me, but this was too much. Soooo much trivia ("ATALANTA"?) but the thing that pissed me off was "ALLOK". That's not a real phrase, it shouldn't be in here.

When I was a child there was a cartoon where a male character says to a female character, "Gee, you're awful pretty". The female responds , "You're pretty awful yourself."  For the Erotic Artist's work to be barely seen, the viewer needs to be either 1) nude while viewing, or 2) almost blind.

AM I OK? Apparently not. An awful lot of phrases, as opposed to long words, which somehow feels like cheating. I accept that it's a legitimate strategy for construction, but it rubs me the wrong way. Well, that's my JAM. Oh, apparently it's not.

I knew that the Jetsons had a robot maid named Rosie, but I didn't remember a "computer friend" of any kind. RUBY perhaps? Apparently not.

Is JIBJAB still a thing? I haven't seen one of those silly animations in a long time, and certainly never received one as an e-card.

Leo Rubinstein famously preferred to be known as Arthur, you're misnaming him the same way Hurok did. You're better than that.

The only pianist Rubinstein I know of with a 5 letter name is Anton.

Everyone’s going to say ARTUR is the proper Polish spelling but well, I still take issue with using it as the answer. I was convinced I was wrong or it was a rebus for a very long time. Not a big fan of clues that go against the grain in this way. Otherwise enjoyed the puzzle.
Great puzzle! I love it when my first pass through all clues looks bleak, but slowly I crack each quadrant. Bring me just to the verge of googling something and then it breaks open.
Zachary, this crossword “served as”  a joke.

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle

a/ here by the light of the fire
before the big oak
here in the cold the light is you
is all the light
d/ of jupiter and red mars…
the noble gases

I read every article on how to solve NYT crosswords, lurked in the comments, studying and learning from the tireless veterans who teach and elucidate.
 
I relied on auto-check and began understanding lateral thinking, figuring out misdirects, using logic and deduction, gaining experience with language patterns.  
I was finally learning this foreign language.

OK - absolutely the most unusual puzzle I've ever encountered; this one all because of the clues. A Sunday from June 23, 2002 by Eric Berlin with the title "Components." Some theme clue examples: 23-across: "69-across component" 9-down: "69-across component" 69 across: "23-across + 9-down" And those answers: 23a: THREEOFAKIND 9d: PAIR and 69a: FULLHOUSE And all the other theme answers were similarly triple-clued. So the only way to get any theme answer would be to work the crosses on the 'components' and the 'answer' until you got to something that made sense. Can't even imagine what that would have been like. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=6/23/2002&g=23&d=A ....

@john ezra

Interesting point about [Computer runs].  

When I started with computers in the early 70s, the only thing you could do with a computer was to "do a run".  You had you code, you had your data, you fed all of it into the computer (or rather the operators took your cards and ran it for you), and you got a printout of the job.  It might diagonalize a matrix, it may do a numerical integration or numerically solve a differential equation, or whatever, but you told the computer what you wanted it to do, and then later got the output.  

Except in scientific circles, I don't think computer runs are "jobs" like that any more.  Today we interact with the computer.  We call up things to read and hear and see with the computer, but it isn't a "job" in the classical computer job sense.

Kind of a throw back answer.

@Francis
What computers did you start with in the 70s? Because mainframes since the 60s have been running JOBS. 

IBM created Job Control Language in the 60s to allow their operating systems to manage the JOBS that users wanted run. It's still used to this day on the IBM z/OS operating system, and every program run on those mainframes is still referred to as a job. 

As for "except in scientific circles", your monthly bank statement is most likely produced by a *job* that's run on a mainframe computer.

commentBody:
@SBK in TO, 

It is clued for Spaghetti Westerns. Those were movies by such as Sergio Leone like his 1964 A Fistful of Dollars and using Italians as extras and sometimes in substantial roles (and often shot in southern Italy or parts of Spain) so the clue was definitely meant to be interpreted as THATA WAY.

@Grumpy

I think that was Francis’ point about a “throw back” answer. You both lay out the reasons why we called them computer “jobs” back then. Like Francis, as an undergrad, I remember standing in line with my stack of 80 character punch cards, usually the night before an assignment was due for me and everyone else in line, waiting to hand them to the grad student behind the counter—praying that there were no typos or do-loops in there somewhere. (Remember being thankful for the “repeat” key on the card punch keyboard?) Today, with apparent real-time interaction with computing for most people, do young-uns still think of submitting computing “jobs”? Maybe professional coders.

@Francis

I don’t know what associations this clue was supposed to activate for non-technical solvers…but “job” is still a ubiquitous term/abstraction in computing nowadays.

Neuralgia translated means "nerve pain". You can have occipital neuralgia (pain of the occipital nerve), trigeminal neuralgia (pain of the trigeminal nerve), glossopharyngeal neuralgia, etc

@Andrzej DITs and dahs seem non5en5ic@l to my English brain too. Just call them dots and dashes people! There's probably a really interesting explanation of why they're not... 

...ok, I looked it up, it's because of the way operators used to vocalise the Morse Code. So they needed short and long syllables, "di dah di dit" (they used di and dit for the short tone, but we'll never see "di" in a crossword...). Hopefully writing this down will forever cement it in my mind.

I am, as I've said, delighted that I've been defeated.

Thank you, Mr. Levy, Will Shortz, and editorial team for today's puzzle! 

With rare exceptions of being on the constructor's wavelength, this is the challenge that an end-of-the-week puzzle should present. 
I will survive the defeat and I will continue to labor at it, happily mingling alongside the veterans who I'm grateful for and no longer need to fear will abscond to greener pastures.

Everyone gets to have their day in the sun.
And this is how it should be.

@sotto voce

You're just the type of person I have been talking about when I have said that if the perceived target audience for whom the puzzles have been made simpler recently sticks with them for just a few years, even they will get bored and move on.

I've been solving for about 45 years, and believe me, in the five years you've been solving, it's been far easier to improve quickly, with answer keys available immediately, the internet to look things up, the Wordplay column and other blogs, and the crossword community who contributes insights and helps newer solvers to understand  how to solve the puzzles. You also have access to multiple outlets on the internet so you can solve several puzzles a day if you choose, plus the Mini and now the Midi from the NYT itself.

When I got started, you literally had to solve in a library to get any help whatsoever, and you had to wait for the next day's paper to see if your solution was right.

Even newcomers should stand up for the right to have solid hard puzzles twice a week every week.  If we don't, they will be gone before they realize.
